did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning classifiers (Naïve Bayes, K-Nearest Neighbor, and Support Vector Machine) to classify dermoscopic images for Melanoma recognition and skin lesion classification. Feature extraction was performed using Spectral Graph Wavelet Transform (SGWT), Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT), Curvelet, and Tetrolet, along with Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for texture features.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a classification method for dermoscopic images using Spectral Graph Wavelet Transform (SGWT) and various classifiers (Naïve Bayes, KNN, SVM). It details a specific methodology, uses the ISIC 2016 dataset, and reports new quantitative results (e.g., AUC, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Analysis and Classification of Dermoscopic Images Using Spectral Graph Wavelet Transform
journal: Periodica Polytechnica Electrical Engineering and Computer Science
brief_summary: This study addresses the recognition of Melanoma and classification of skin lesions using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2016 dataset. The authors propose a method that extracts shape, color, and texture features, with texture features derived from Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and multiresolution transforms including Spectral Graph Wavelet Transform (SGWT), DWT, Curvelet, and Tetrolet. These features are classified using Naïve Bayes, K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers. The proposed method utilizing SGWT achieved an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.951, with an accuracy of 96.79%, sensitivity of 88%, and specificity of 98.26% for Melanoma recognition. For multi-class skin lesion classification, SGWT demonstrated superior performance, achieving AUC values ranging from 0.895 to 0.98 across various lesion pairs. The results indicate that SGWT effectively handles irregular skin lesion shapes, outperforming conventional wavelet transforms in specificity and accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for recognition task; 2169 images in ISIC 2016 dataset; classified against Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Basal Cell Carcinoma.
Benign comparator class; 18566 images in ISIC 2016 dataset; used in Melanoma vs Nevus and Nevus vs Basal Cell Carcinoma classification pairs.
Malignant comparator class; 586 images in ISIC 2016 dataset; used in Melanoma vs BCC and Nevus vs BCC classification pairs.
Benign comparator class; 419 images in ISIC 2016 dataset; used in Seborrheic Keratosis vs SCC and Melanoma vs SK classification pairs.
Malignant comparator class; 226 images in ISIC 2016 dataset; used in Seborrheic Keratosis vs SCC classification pair.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (Melanoma vs. non-Melanoma; and multi-class skin lesion pairs including Melanoma vs Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis vs Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma vs Seborrheic Keratosis, Melanoma vs Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Nevus vs Basal Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification for Melanoma recognition and multi-class classification for skin lesion pairs using Naïve Bayes, K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers. Performance is evaluated using Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and AUC. Although segmentation is performed as a preprocessing step using region growing, the primary AI task reported is classification of the extracted features.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016 Archive (ISBI Challenge 2016)
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images and metadata from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration archive, used for Melanoma recognition and skin lesion classification. Images vary in size from 4288 × 2848 to 722 × 542.

use.training: Used for Melanoma recognition (900 training images) and skin lesion classification tasks.
use.testing: Used for Melanoma recognition (379 test images) and skin lesion classification tasks.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To standardize the feature vector comprising Shape, Color, and Texture features before classification, as the feature set contained a mixture of positive and negative values.
To calculate Sensitivity and Specificity values for different transform techniques and classifiers.
To evaluate and compare the performance of Multiresolution transforms (DWT, Curvelet, Tetrolet, SGWT) for Melanoma recognition and multi-class skin lesion classification.
To evaluate the classification performance of Naïve Bayes, KNN, and SVM classifiers on the ISIC 2016 dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and Area Under the Curve (AUC).

ISIC 2016 Archive (Melanoma Recognition).description: Binary classification of Melanoma vs Non-Melanoma using Shape, Color, and Texture features extracted via various transforms and classified with Naïve Bayes, SVM (various kernels), and KNN.
ISIC 2016 Archive (Melanoma Recognition).notes: SGWT with KNN achieved the highest accuracy (96.7912%), sensitivity (88%), and specificity (98.26%). SGWT also achieved the highest AUC (0.951) among all transforms. The combination of Shape + Color + Texture features yielded the highest accuracy (96.7912%) compared to other feature combinations (Shape+Color: 92.2621%, Texture+Color: 94.231%, Shape+Texture: 95.1163%). Sensitivity/Specificity for SGWT were 88%/98.26%, compared to Tetrolet (86%/97%), Curvelet (86%/96%), and DWT (85%/95%).

ISIC 2016 Archive (Multi-class Skin Lesi.description: Binary classification of specific skin lesion pairs using Multiresolution transforms (DWT, Curvelet, Tetrolet, SGWT) and KNN classifier.
ISIC 2016 Archive (Multi-class Skin Lesi.splits: Not explicitly defined per pair, but derived from the ISIC 2016 dataset classes.
ISIC 2016 Archive (Multi-class Skin Lesi.notes: SGWT generally outperformed other transforms in AUC for most pairs. For Nevus vs Basal Cell Carcinoma, SGWT achieved 98% accuracy. For Seborrheic Keratosis vs Squamous Cell Carcinoma, SGWT had an AUC of 0.945. The text notes that for SK vs SCC, Curvelet had the highest sensitivity (0.8482) but SGWT dominated in specificity and accuracy (89.5%).

architecture_used.main_model: Traditional machine learning classifiers (Naïve Bayes, K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM)) combined with hand-crafted feature extraction using Spectral Graph Wavelet Transform (SGWT), Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT), Curvelet, and Tetrolet.
architecture_used.input_size: Variable image dimensions ranging from 4288 × 2848 to 722 × 542 pixels.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Shape, Color, and Texture features. Texture features derived from Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and coefficients of multiresolution transforms (SGWT, DWT, Curvelet, Tetrolet).
architecture_details.segmentation: Region growing technique applied after preprocessing with a weighted average filter to remove artifacts.
architecture_details.preprocessing: Z-Score standardization of the feature vector.

training_details.dataset_split: 80-20 train-test split (900 training images, 379 testing images for Melanoma recognition).

classifier_configs.Naive_Bayes: Assumes features are independent of given class.

training_details.implementation_environment: MATLAB R2017a Version No 9.2 on 2.30 GHz Intel Core i5 processor.

preprocessing_techniques: Weighted average filter applied to remove acquisition artifacts (rulers, ink marks, reflections, air bubbles) and cutaneous artifacts (skin lines, blood vessels, thin lines)
Region growing technique for segmentation of skin lesions/tumors
Z-Score standardization of the feature vector (comprising Shape, Color, and Texture features) to handle mixed positive and negative values
conclusions: The proposed method using Spectral Graph Wavelet Transform (SGWT) for feature extraction outperforms conventional multiresolution transforms (DWT, Curvelet, Tetrolet) in Melanoma recognition, achieving an AUC of 0.951, accuracy of 96.79%, sensitivity of 88%, and specificity of 98.26%.

SGWT is superior to conventional wavelet transforms because it effectively handles irregularly shaped skin lesions by using weighted graphs (meshes) as its base, allowing it to capture connectivity data embedded in edge weights rather than relying solely on vertex features.
The combination of Shape, Color, and Texture features yields higher classification accuracy (96.7912%) compared to any pairwise combination of these feature types.
Among the classifiers tested (Naïve Bayes, SVM, KNN), the KNN classifier combined with SGWT features achieved the highest accuracy (96.7912%) for Melanoma recognition.

For multi-class skin lesion classification, SGWT demonstrated superior performance with AUC values ranging from 0.895 to 0.98 across various lesion pairs (Melanoma vs Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis vs Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma vs Seborrheic Keratosis, Melanoma vs Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Nevus vs Basal Cell Carcinoma).
In the classification of Seborrheic Keratosis vs Squamous Cell Carcinoma, while Curvelet had the highest sensitivity, SGWT dominated in specificity and accuracy (89.5%).
The classification of Nevus vs Basal Cell Carcinoma using SGWT achieved an accuracy of 98%, which was higher than accuracies obtained using other transforms.

The computational time for texture feature extraction varies significantly by transform: DWT (0.322s), Curvelet (4.298s), Tetrolet (4.028s), and SGWT (14.11s), indicating that while SGWT offers superior accuracy, it is computationally more expensive than DWT.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC 2016 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 18,566 Nevus vs. 226 Squamous Cell Carcinoma), potentially biasing performance metrics towards the majority class.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (p-values) or confidence intervals for the reported performance metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, AUC).
The methodology uses a simple 80-20 train-test split without cross-validation, which may lead to high variance in performance estimates and limits the robustness of the evaluation.

There is a risk of data leakage or overfitting as the segmentation (region growing) and feature extraction are performed on the entire dataset before splitting, and no external validation set is used.

The computational cost for the proposed SGWT method is significantly higher (14.11 seconds) compared to DWT (0.322 seconds), which may hinder real-time clinical deployment despite its superior accuracy.

The paper compares its results against a limited set of existing literature (Table 8) rather than providing a comprehensive benchmark against state-of-the-art deep learning models on the same dataset split.
The dataset contains images of varying sizes (4288 × 2848 to 722 × 542), but the paper does not detail how this variability was handled during feature extraction or if resizing introduced artifacts.
The study focuses on binary classification pairs for multi-class tasks, which does not fully capture the complexity of multi-class classification problems in clinical settings.
